subject: everyday-science
description: Rocks & Types MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: Sedimentary rock is best described as a rock formed:
options: Deep in Earth's mantle by extreme pressure
At or near the Earth's surface by the accumulation and lithification of sediment, or precipitation from solution
mcqs.explanation: Sedimentary rock is formed at or near the Earth's surface by the accumulation and lithification of sediment or by the precipitation from solution at normal surface temperatures.

mcqs.q: Artesian wells are found in which type of rocks?
mcqs.explanation: Artesian wells are found in sedimentary rocks — porous, water-bearing layers between impermeable beds.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT a sedimentary rock?
mcqs.explanation: Marble is a metamorphic rock (formed from limestone). Sandstone, limestone and shale are sedimentary.

mcqs.q: Sedimentary rocks make up:
options: Only a tiny fraction of Earth's surface
Most of the rocks on the Earth
mcqs.explanation: Sedimentary rocks make up most of the rocks on the Earth — they cover about three-quarters of Earth's surface.

mcqs.q: Earth's crust below the oceans is composed mainly of:
mcqs.explanation: Earth's crust below the oceans is composed of sedimentary rocks.

mcqs.q: Coal is what type of rock?
options: Organic sedimentary rock formed mainly from plant debris
mcqs.explanation: Coal is an organic sedimentary rock that forms mainly from plant debris compressed over millions of years.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is an example of a sedimentary rock?
mcqs.explanation: Sandstone is a sedimentary rock. Granite is intrusive igneous, marble is metamorphic, and basalt is extrusive igneous.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT typically classified as a sedimentary rock?
mcqs.explanation: Slate is a metamorphic rock (metamorphosed shale). Limestone, conglomerate and coquina are all sedimentary.

mcqs.q: Which is an example of an organic sedimentary rock?
mcqs.explanation: Coal is an organic sedimentary rock formed primarily from compressed plant remains.

mcqs.q: Breccia, chalk limestone, conglomerate and shale are all examples of which rock type?
mcqs.explanation: Breccia, chalk limestone, conglomerate, sandstone, dolomites, gypsum, coal, shale, siltstone and coquina are all sedimentary rocks.

mcqs.q: Metamorphic rocks form when rocks are subjected to:
options: High heat, high pressure or hot mineral-rich fluids
mcqs.explanation: Metamorphic rocks form when existing rocks are subjected to high heat, high pressure or hot mineral-rich fluids that change their structure and mineralogy.

mcqs.q: Amphibolite is a coarse-grained metamorphic rock composed mainly of:
options: Green/brown/black amphibole minerals and plagioclase feldspar
mcqs.explanation: Amphibolite is a coarse-grained metamorphic rock composed mainly of green, brown or black amphibole minerals and plagioclase feldspar.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a metamorphic rock?
mcqs.explanation: Marble is a metamorphic rock (formed from metamorphosed limestone). Sandstone is sedimentary, granite is intrusive igneous, basalt is extrusive igneous.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT a metamorphic rock?
mcqs.explanation: Sandstone is a sedimentary rock. Slate, quartzite and gneiss are all metamorphic rocks.

mcqs.q: Soapstone is an example of which type of rock?
mcqs.explanation: Soapstone is a metamorphic rock — composed mostly of talc.

mcqs.q: Examples of metamorphic rocks include:
options: Blue schist, eclogite, gneiss, marble, quartzite, slate
mcqs.explanation: Examples of metamorphic rocks: blue schist, eclogite, gneiss, greenstone, marble, quartzite and slate.

mcqs.q: Marble is formed by the metamorphism of which rock?
mcqs.explanation: Marble is formed by the metamorphism of limestone — heat and pressure recrystallise calcium carbonate into the dense, crystalline marble.

mcqs.q: Igneous rocks are also known as:
mcqs.explanation: Igneous rocks are also known as magmatic rocks because they form from molten magma.

mcqs.q: Igneous rocks form when:
mcqs.explanation: Igneous rocks form when magma (molten rock) cools and crystallises — either underground or on the surface.

mcqs.q: Igneous rocks are divided into how many main groups?
mcqs.explanation: Igneous rocks are divided into two main groups: intrusive (plutonic) and extrusive (volcanic).

mcqs.q: Intrusive igneous rocks form:
options: Within Earth's crust as molten material rises and hardens
mcqs.explanation: Intrusive igneous rock forms within Earth's crust — the molten material rises, fills voids or melts overlying rocks, and eventually hardens slowly underground.

mcqs.q: Intrusive rocks are also called:
mcqs.explanation: Intrusive rocks are also called plutonic rocks — named after Pluto, the Roman god of the underworld, since they form deep beneath Earth's surface.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is an example of an intrusive igneous rock?
mcqs.explanation: Granite is an intrusive (plutonic) igneous rock. Examples include pegmatite, diabase, diorite, granite and peridotite.

mcqs.q: Extrusive igneous rocks form when:
options: Magma (called lava once it reaches the surface) flows onto Earth's surface
mcqs.explanation: Extrusive igneous rocks form when magma — called lava once it reaches the surface — flows onto Earth's surface and cools rapidly.

mcqs.q: Extrusive rocks are also known as:
mcqs.explanation: Extrusive rocks are also known as volcanic rocks — they form from lava on Earth's surface.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is an example of an extrusive igneous rock?
mcqs.explanation: Basalt is an extrusive (volcanic) igneous rock. Examples include andesite, basalt, dacite, obsidian and tuff.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT an example of an extrusive igneous rock?
mcqs.explanation: Granite is an INTRUSIVE (plutonic) igneous rock. Andesite, basalt and obsidian are all extrusive.

mcqs.q: Pegmatite, diorite and peridotite are examples of:
mcqs.explanation: Pegmatite, diabase, diorite, granite and peridotite are examples of intrusive (plutonic) igneous rocks.

mcqs.q: Andesite, dacite, obsidian and tuff are examples of:
mcqs.explanation: Andesite, basalt, dacite, obsidian and tuff are examples of extrusive (volcanic) igneous rocks.

mcqs.q: What is the difference between magma and lava?
options: Magma is below Earth's surface; lava is magma that has reached the surface
Lava is below; magma is above
mcqs.explanation: Magma is molten rock below Earth's surface. Once it is ejected onto the surface (e.g. by a volcano), the same material is called lava.

mcqs.q: Which group of igneous rocks cools more slowly and produces larger crystals?
mcqs.explanation: Intrusive (plutonic) rocks cool slowly underground, allowing larger crystals to form (e.g. granite). Extrusive rocks cool quickly on the surface and form smaller crystals or glass (e.g. obsidian).

mcqs.q: What is the hydrosphere?
options: A discontinuous layer of water at or near Earth's surface
mcqs.explanation: The hydrosphere is a discontinuous layer of water at or near Earth's surface — it includes all liquid and frozen surface waters, groundwater held in soil and rock, and atmospheric water vapour.

mcqs.q: What is the most abundant substance at the surface of Earth?
mcqs.explanation: Water is the most abundant substance at the surface of Earth — covering ~71% of it.

mcqs.q: Approximately how much water (in liquid and frozen form) makes up Earth's hydrosphere?
mcqs.explanation: About 1.4 billion cubic km (326 million cubic miles) of water in liquid and frozen form make up the oceans, lakes, streams, glaciers and groundwaters.

mcqs.q: The Kuroshio is a:
options: Warm north-easterly ocean current off the coast of Japan
mcqs.explanation: The Kuroshio is a warm north-easterly ocean current off the coast of Japan — also called the Black Current.

mcqs.q: The Benguela Current is the eastern boundary current of which ocean?
mcqs.explanation: The Benguela Current is the eastern boundary current of the South Atlantic Ocean — it flows northward along the western coast of southern Africa.

mcqs.q: In the northern hemisphere, the north-easterly winter monsoon winds become which direction in summer?
mcqs.explanation: The north-easterly direction of winter monsoon winds becomes south-westerly during the summer season in the northern hemisphere.

mcqs.q: How often do the currents in the northern Indian Ocean change their flow direction?
options: Twice a year (due to NE and SW monsoon winds)
mcqs.explanation: The currents in the northern Indian Ocean change their flow direction twice a year due to the NE and SW monsoon winds.

mcqs.q: What is the average salinity of seawater?
mcqs.explanation: On average, seawater in the world's oceans has a salinity of approximately 3.5%, or 34–35 parts per thousand.

mcqs.q: In the ocean, water moves horizontally over vast areas mainly due to:
mcqs.explanation: In the ocean, water moves horizontally over vast areas due to density difference (thermohaline circulation).

mcqs.q: What is a Spring tide?
options: Tide that occurs only in spring season
mcqs.explanation: A Spring tide is the tide at its maximum height — occurring at new and full moons when the Sun, Earth and Moon are aligned.

mcqs.q: What is a glacier?
options: A type of warm ocean current
A huge mass of ice that moves slowly over land
mcqs.explanation: A glacier is a huge mass of ice that moves slowly over land — formed from compacted snow over many years.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT included in Earth's hydrosphere?
mcqs.explanation: Magma is molten rock, not water — it belongs to the lithosphere/mantle, not the hydrosphere. The hydrosphere includes liquid water, ice and atmospheric water vapour.
